Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study in the evenings after work, but at 29 with everything else going on, I often ended up just listening to lecture recordings while doing other things around the house. I didn't have a strict schedule—more like I'd fit studying into whatever gaps I could find. Sometimes I'd have a productive weekend, other times weeks would slip by without much happening.
Q2: It definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was happening in my life. I have extracurriculars and other commitments that sometimes took priority. When an assignment was due, I'd cram, but otherwise my pattern was pretty inconsistent. I kept meaning to establish a regular schedule but it never really stuck.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder email or realizing I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in because I felt guilty about falling behind. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me to log in a few extra times—earning points or badges gave me a small reason to check in even when I wasn't feeling motivated. But honestly, most of the time it was deadline panic that got me there.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried to make it a habit early on, but it never became automatic for me. There was a brief period when the gamification elements made it feel more like checking a game than doing coursework, which was kind of nice. But that novelty wore off and I went back to only logging in when I absolutely had to. Routine just isn't my strong suit with online learning.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded discussions were most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I could put them on while commuting or doing chores, which fit my lifestyle better than sitting and reading. The discussion boards occasionally had useful insights from other students too. Anything I could absorb passively was valuable because I could multitask.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and the longer video content. If something was over 30 minutes, I'd put it off indefinitely. The discussion posts felt like busywork—I only contributed twice the whole course. I also delayed the optional practice quizzes because they didn't count toward my grade and I was already stretched thin.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to passing the course or the exam, it felt worth my time. Activities that were quick, gave me immediate feedback, or had some kind of reward attached—like the gamified tasks—felt more worthwhile. Anything that seemed like it would take forever with unclear benefits got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With required ones, there's external pressure—you have to do them or you fail. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always lost out to other demands on my time. I'd think 'I'll get to that later' and later never came. The gamified optional tasks were slightly different because they had that reward element, but even those I didn't fully engage with.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio files and listened to them offline during my commute or at the gym. I also used some external YouTube videos and podcasts related to the course topics because sometimes they explained things better. My notes were all in a physical notebook that I'd review away from my computer. A lot of my actual learning happened unplugged.
Q10: There were many times I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times while doing other things, but that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd also discuss course concepts with a friend who took a similar course years ago—we'd talk through ideas over coffee. Reviewing my handwritten notes before the exam was a big part of my prep, but the LMS would have no record of any of that.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd download the audio lectures or save PDFs and then work through them on my own time without going back to the LMS. Once I had the material, I didn't see the point of logging back in just to access it again. My engagement with those materials continued, but the system wouldn't know that.
Q12: Around midterm season, things got really hard. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars and some personal stuff, and the course just slipped down my priority list. My anxiety spiked a bit during that time, and I felt overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind. It became easier to avoid the LMS than to face the backlog of things I hadn't done.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big factor—after a full day, I often didn't have the mental energy to engage properly. When I was confused about something, I'd sometimes just close the laptop rather than push through. My other responsibilities always seemed more urgent than coursework that wasn't immediately due. The LMS became something I avoided when life got busy.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video, but my mind was elsewhere. I was going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open in a tab while doing something else entirely. Those sessions probably looked like engagement but they were pretty hollow.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was anxiety—starting felt overwhelming, so I'd put it off. Part of it was just that other things felt more pressing or more interesting. I'd tell myself I work better under pressure, but really I was just avoiding discomfort. My assignment completion rate was abysmal because of this.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, then go quiet again. Without a deadline looming, I had no urgency. The LMS probably shows these spikes clearly—nothing for days, then sudden frantic activity. It's not a healthy pattern but it's how I operated.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but I didn't always seek it out promptly. When I did get feedback on assignments, if it was positive I'd feel briefly motivated, but that faded quickly. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage more because it confirmed I was behind. I wish I'd been better at using feedback constructively, but my resilience for setbacks isn't great.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but was actually hard for me. Too much freedom meant I could always put things off. The weekly modules didn't create enough urgency for someone like me who needs external pressure. The gamified elements helped a bit by adding structure, but overall the flexibility worked against my tendency to drift.
Q19: The audio options made engagement easier since that's how I learn best. But the sheer number of courses I was juggling—seven online courses—made it hard to give any single one proper attention. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't match how I naturally engage. Having more structured checkpoints or mandatory pacing might have helped me stay on track.
Q20: I think that could be someone exploring, trying to figure out what's important, maybe feeling lost. They might be logging in with good intentions but not knowing where to focus. Or they could be like me on bad days—present in body but not in mind, clicking around without purpose. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me to some extent. I passed with decent exam scores despite low LMS activity because I did a lot of learning offline. Listening to downloaded materials, studying from notes, using external resources—none of that shows up in the system. Someone can be learning effectively without the LMS capturing it.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, how long pages were open. They're good at capturing patterns of access. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether you were actually thinking or just scrolling. They miss offline study, external resources, conversations with peers, and the mental and emotional state you were in while studying.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being present and curious. For me, real engagement happened in moments when I was genuinely interested, usually while listening to something that clicked. Those moments were rare this course, honestly.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was dealing with moderate anxiety and low resilience, which affected everything. It wouldn't capture my offline listening habits or the conversations I had about course material. It wouldn't know that I was juggling seven courses and had a life outside of school. And it definitely wouldn't show that despite appearing disengaged, I actually wanted to do well—I just struggled to translate that into consistent action.
